stem: A retail study measures customer satisfaction score across 4 chains, each containing multiple stores. chain A has 15 observations with mean 65.0 /10 (within-chain SD = 6.8). chain B has 5 observations with mean 100.7 /10 (within-chain SD = 4.0). chain C has 5 observations with mean 99.6 /10 (within-chain SD = 8.8). chain D has 10 observations with mean 82.9 /10 (within-chain SD = 4.7). The between-chain standard deviation is uncertain, estimated between 5.9 and 14.9 /10. Using a hierarchical Bayesian model, estimate the true customer satisfaction score for chain B, accounting for shrinkage toward the grand mean and the uncertainty in the between-chain variation.

ground_truth.answer: Hierarchical estimate for chain B: 100.1 /10 (shrunk from raw 100.7). Range [99.0, 100.4] as between-chain SD varies from 5.9 to 14.9. Pooled mean: 80.2.

solution_steps: Compute pooled (grand) mean: 80.2 /10 (weighted average of 4 chain means).
Shrinkage factor for chain B: B = σ²_between / (σ²_between + σ²_within/5). At σ_between=9.9: B = 0.968.
Interval [99.0, 100.4] captures uncertainty from the unknown between-group variance.
common_failure_modes: Using the raw observed mean (100.7) without shrinkage
Using only the grand mean (80.2) and ignoring group-specific data
Not recognizing that small-sample groups should be shrunk more toward the grand mean
Treating the between-group variance as known rather than uncertain
